critique.summary: The article provides an in-depth overview of the new features and improvements introduced in Java 26, including enhancements in garbage collection, HTTP/3 support, structured concurrency, lazy constants, and pattern matching for primitive types. It also discusses deprecations and preparations for future Java projects such as Project Valhalla.
mainPoints: Java 26 introduces new HotSpot JVM features like ahead-of-time object caching compatible with any garbage collector and improved G1 GC throughput by reducing synchronization.
HTTP Client API now supports HTTP/3 with multiple negotiation strategies to handle varying server capabilities.
Structured concurrency is enhanced to provide better task hierarchy, cancellation, and error propagation, improving concurrent programming models.
Lazy constants are introduced to allow deferred, thread-safe initialization of immutable fields, improving startup performance and concurrency safety.
Pattern matching is extended to support primitive types in instanceof and switch constructs, enhancing language expressiveness.
Several features are re-previewed with minor changes, and JEP 500 prepares the platform to restrict mutation of final fields via deep reflection for improved correctness and performance.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately summarizes the official Java Enhancement Proposals (JEPs) and features introduced in Java 26 as documented by the OpenJDK project and related authoritative sources. The technical explanations align with publicly available JEP documents and Java release notes. The article provides detailed descriptions and code examples consistent with the official Java documentation, indicating a high level of factual accuracy.
considerations: The article assumes familiarity with Java internals and JEP terminology, which may limit accessibility for beginners.
It does not discuss potential drawbacks or challenges of adopting new features, such as migration issues or compatibility concerns.
The preview status of several features means they are subject to change and may not be stable for production use yet.
The article does not provide perspectives from independent Java developers or industry experts on the impact of these changes.
Performance implications are discussed mostly in idealized terms; real-world results may vary depending on application context.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around recent Java developments, particularly Java 26, with comments on the removal of outdated APIs, the evolution of Java features like virtual threads, and the cultural challenges within the Java community. There is also curiosity about Android's lag in adopting new Java versions and some criticism of Google's approach to Java in the Android ecosystem.

comments.author: xeubie
comments.text:
I think astronomers could measure the age of the universe in nano-Valhallas. Every year, it feels 50% closer to completion...

In all seriousness I'm happy with what Mr. Goetz and the team have done. Sealed interfaces (java 17) + exhaustive switch statements (java 21) means we now have union types in java! And instead of jumping on the async/await bandwagon we now have a more general solution that doesn't lead to API duplication (virtual threads). But Valhalla has been a veeery long time coming.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately highlights recent Java language features and provides a thoughtful comparison to async/await, reflecting a good understanding of Java's evolution.

comments.author: vyskocilm
comments.text:
JEP 504: Remove the Applet API

Glad to see this being removed. Java plugins especially on Linux were awful and required by tons of corporate stuff. Anyone remeber IcedTea Web? A functional and opensource Java plugin and Java Webstart implementation?
comments.analysis: The removal of the Applet API is a well-known fact and this comment correctly notes the historical issues with Java plugins, making it accurate and relevant.

comments.author: dzonga
comments.text:
the people that work on Java & the JVM are very smart.

it has become a best of breed language - hell its better than Go for industry purposes.

the drawback with Java will always be the CULTURE - (maybe someone can insert a quote of how in physics progress is only made, when old physicist die - I don't wanna be morbid ) but with Java same that's when the culture will change.

All those people using typescript (could be using Java - but the culture doesn't want them and consider them heretics for not embracing religion of OOP and FactoryFactory)
comments.analysis: The comment provides a subjective but insightful cultural critique of the Java community, which is a valid perspective though somewhat speculative.

comments.author: freedomben
comments.text: As someone who has been out of the Java world for many years, but recently forced back into it due to Android dev requirements, Post lawsuit, what is the relationship between Android (Google) and Java now?  When can we expect 26 on Android?  On that note, why is Android always so far behind?  Is it because Kotlin is primary or is it deeper?  Did the lawsuit play a role?
comments.analysis: This comment raises relevant questions about Android's Java support and the impact of historical legal issues, reflecting common concerns though without definitive answers.

comments.author: haolez
comments.text: I was pretty surprised when I learned recently that the Java alternative for green threads doesn't use colored functions. It put Java in a higher place in my perception.
comments.analysis: The comment correctly notes Java's approach to virtual threads and expresses a personal positive impression, which is accurate but anecdotal.

comments.author: badgersnake
comments.text: Vector API (Eleventh Incubator) - maybe 11th time’s the charm.
comments.analysis: This comment humorously notes the repeated incubation of the Vector API, which is factual but brief and less substantive.

comments.author: wiseowise
comments.text: Android as always in shambles. Shame that neither Google, nor ecosystem cares about Java because they’ve bought JetBrains kopium.
comments.analysis: This comment is emotionally charged and somewhat speculative, using slang and insults without providing constructive or factual support.
